era_context: The Roman Empire under Constantius II, with Julian recently appointed as Caesar of Gaul (355 CE). This year marks the beginning of Julian's tenure as Caesar and the start of his military campaigns against Germanic tribes. The empire faced simultaneous threats from Germanic tribes in the north and Sasanian Persia in the east, while theological disputes continued to fracture Christian unity.

events.title: Julian begins military campaigns in Gaul as Caesar
events.description: Julian, newly appointed as Caesar of Gaul in 355 CE, began military campaigns against Germanic tribes, particularly the Alamanni and Franks, who had been raiding Roman territory. These early campaigns were part of a broader effort to stabilize the Rhine frontier and restore Roman military control. Julian's campaigns were initially successful, and his military reputation grew rapidly. The campaigns would continue and intensify over the next few years, culminating in his proclamation as Augustus in 360 CE.
key_figures: Germanic tribal leaders (names not consistently recorded)
events.certainty_note: Julian's appointment as Caesar and his military campaigns in Gaul are well-attested. The date of his appointment is secure (355 CE), and his campaigns in 356 CE are documented in multiple sources.

events.title: Constantius II campaigns in the Danube region
events.description: Constantius II conducted military campaigns in the Danube region to counter threats from Germanic and other barbarian tribes. These campaigns were part of a broader effort to stabilize the empire's northern frontier. The campaigns were moderately successful but did not permanently resolve the barbarian threat. Constantius II's military efforts on the Danube frontier were concurrent with his efforts to address Sasanian pressure on the eastern frontier.
key_figures: Barbarian tribal leaders (names not consistently recorded)
events.certainty_note: Constantius II's military activity on the Danube frontier is attested, but the precise dating and outcome of campaigns within 356 CE are somewhat unclear. Ammianus provides accounts, but his narrative is complex and sometimes difficult to parse chronologically.

events.title: Athanasius of Alexandria exiled again
events.description: Athanasius of Alexandria, the prominent Christian bishop and theologian, was exiled again by Constantius II in 356 CE. Athanasius had been a leading figure in the Nicene controversy and opposed the Arian theology favored by Constantius II. His exile was part of Constantius II's broader effort to impose doctrinal uniformity on the Christian church. Athanasius would spend much of the remainder of Constantius II's reign in exile, returning to Alexandria after Constantius's death in 361 CE.

sources.name: Athanasius of Alexandria, writings on his exile
Eusebius of Caesarea and later church historians

events.certainty_note: Athanasius's exile in 356 CE is well-attested in multiple sources. Athanasius himself wrote about his exile, and later church historians provide accounts. The date is secure.
cross_references: 361-003 (Athanasius's return to Alexandria after Constantius II's death)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Julian's early military campaigns in Gaul is sometimes overstated; while they were successful, they did not permanently resolve the Germanic threat to the Rhine frontier. Additionally, the extent to which Constantius II's campaigns on the Danube frontier were effective is disputed; some sources suggest they were largely unsuccessful. The dating of Athanasius's exile is sometimes placed in 356 CE or 357 CE depending on the source; some sources suggest he was exiled multiple times during this period.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 356 CE is dominated by Ammianus Marcellinus, whose account is detailed but contains some chronological ambiguities. Church historians provide accounts of religious developments, particularly Athanasius's exile. Sasanian sources are limited to inscriptions and later compilations. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; economic, social, and cultural history are largely absent. Non-Mediterranean regions are virtually absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Julian's military campaigns in 356 CE led to his continued success and the Battle of Strasbourg in 357 or 358 CE
Constantius II's military efforts on the Danube frontier were concurrent with Sasanian pressure on the eastern frontier
Athanasius's exile in 356 CE was reversed after Constantius II's death in 361 CE
